g.text: Foundation of Event Ordering in Distributed Systems

g.data-narration: Physical clocks drift and cannot be perfectly synchronized. Network delays make accurate timestamps impossible in distributed systems.
g.text: Why Do We Need Logical Time?
Physical clocks have drift and cannot be perfectly synchronized across distributed systems.
NTP has network delays; no perfect global time exists in asynchronous systems.

g.data-narration: Logical time assigns timestamps based on causal relationships between events, not physical occurrence. It captures what happened-before what.
g.text: causal relationships between events, not physical time.

g.data-narration: The happened-before relation defines causal ordering with three rules: same-process order, message send-before-receive, and transitivity.
g.text: Three fundamental rules define causal ordering:
If a→b in same process, a→b
Notation: a → b means "a happened-before b"
Concurrent: a || b if neither a→b nor b→a

g.data-narration: Lamport logical clocks are scalar counters. Each process maintains its own counter and increments it before executing any event.
g.text: Each process Pi maintains a scalar counter Ci. The clock satisfies:
If a → b then L(a) < L(b)

g.data-narration: Lamport clock updates follow three rules: increment before local event, attach timestamp when sending, and sync-on-receive using max function.
Let's trace Lamport clocks through a three-process message-passing scenario. Watch how timestamps propagate and sync across processes.
Lamport clocks provide total ordering but cannot detect concurrency. If timestamps are ordered, events may still be concurrent.
g.text: If a → b then L(a) < L(b)
Events can be globally ordered by timestamp
L(a) < L(b) does NOT imply a → b
Cannot distinguish concurrent from causally ordered

g.data-narration: Vector clocks solve the concurrency detection problem. Each process maintains a vector of all process clocks, capturing complete causal history.
g.text: Each process maintains a vector of n counters (one per process):

g.data-narration: Vector clock updates: increment your own index on local event, take element-wise max on receive, then increment your index again.
Vector clocks compare element-wise: VC(a) < VC(b) if all elements are less-or-equal and at least one is strictly less. Otherwise, concurrent.
g.text: All elements ≤, at least one strictly <

g.data-narration: Let's trace vector clocks through message passing. Watch how vectors merge knowledge and track causal dependencies precisely.
Logical clocks enable distributed mutual exclusion, checkpointing, and conflict detection in replicated databases like Amazon Dynamo.
Review the essential formulas: Lamport clock update rules, vector clock comparison, and the key property linking happened-before with logical time.
g.text: a → b ⇒ L(a) < L(b) AND VC(a) < VC(b)

g.data-narration: Remember: send happens-before receive, use max not min on receive, and vector clocks can detect concurrency while Lamport clocks cannot.
